title: Gauge Geometry of Hodge Zero-Mode Transport in Parameter-Dependent Topological Data Analysis
authors: Satoshi Kanno, Rei Nishimura, Hiroshi Yamauchi, Yoshi-aki Shimada
abstract: We propose a practical computational framework for detecting structural changes in parameter-dependent topological data. In many applications, such as time-series data analysis, anomaly detection, and monitoring of systems under changing control parameters, persistence diagrams describe the birth and death of topological features at each parameter value, but they do not fully capture how these features are reorganized over time. To address this limitation, we represent homological features by zero modes of the ordinary combinatorial Hodge Laplacian and track the corresponding feature spaces in a common ambient chain space. This allows us to compute curvature and holonomy as descriptors of local reorganization and accumulated memory in evolving topological structures. Curvature highlights parameter regions where homological features mix or change rapidly, while holonomy summarizes the net effect of such changes after a closed cycle. We also establish stability estimates showing that these descriptors are robust under perturbations of the Hodge Laplacian on regular regions. Numerical experiments on controlled time-dependent point-cloud data show that the proposed method detects tracking instability, distinguishes systems with nearly identical persistence diagrams, and captures cycle-level memory invisible to pointwise feature matching. These results suggest that zero-mode transport geometry can serve as a useful computational tool for analyzing dynamic topological data.
summary: This paper introduces a new way to track how topological features in data evolve over time or external parameters. By treating homology as a geometric bundle, it uses concepts like curvature and holonomy to detect structural changes that traditional methods miss.
main_problem: Standard Topological Data Analysis (TDA) tools like persistence diagrams fail to capture how homological feature spaces reorganize, mix, or transport when data depends on external parameters like time or control variables.
main_result: The authors develop a gauge-geometric framework using the Hodge Laplacian to describe the transport of homology, proving that curvature and holonomy are stable descriptors of structural reorganization in regular parameter regions.
method: The framework uses the combinatorial Hodge Laplacian to represent homology as a zero-mode bundle, applying Berry-type connection, curvature, and holonomy to track feature evolution.
model_or_system: A parameter-dependent family of simplicial complexes (e.g., Vietoris-Rips filtrations) where the topology is studied as a vector bundle over a parameter space (time, temperature, or external fields).
key_observables: Curvature (local reorganization), Holonomy (accumulated memory/monodromy), and zero-mode projections.
important_parameters: Filtration scale (a), external parameter (lambda), spectral gap (gamma), and smoothing parameter (epsilon).
main_assumptions: The theory assumes 'regular regions' where the zero-mode multiplicity is constant and a spectral gap exists; it also uses a smooth approximation for the discrete Laplacian.
figures_summary: The figures likely include curvature heatmaps identifying reorganization regions, comparisons between vineyard-style tracking and holonomy-guided tracking, and stability plots showing error bounds under noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the limitations of existing TDA, constructs a smooth Hodge-type operator and a zero-mode vector bundle, defines geometric quantities (connection, curvature, holonomy), provides stability proofs via Riesz projection, and validates the method through numerical experiments on time-dependent point clouds.
why_interesting: The use of Berry connection, curvature, and holonomy to describe the evolution of topological features is directly analogous to the study of geometric phases and topological invariants in many-body systems and driven quantum dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Vineyard-like tracking used in Experiment 1. The resulting vineyard-like tracking, including the elder vine and crossing windows.
Fig. 2 Endpoint permutation and holonomy in Experiment 1. One loop sends (v1, v2, v3) to (v2, v3, v1), and the zero-mode holonomy gives the same permutation matrix.
Fig. 3 Dense snapshots over one period for Experiment 3, approaching double circles.
Fig. 4 Vineyard-style tracking of the top two persistence points in Experiment 2.

Fig. 5 Time series of three quantities in Experiment 2: the distance between the top two persistence points, the tracking ambiguity margin, and the maximum curvature. The distance and ambiguity margin become nearly zero at the swap time, while the curvature attains its peak in the same region.
